title: Mpemba Effect in Parametrically Driven Coupled Oscillators under White and Colored Noise
authors: Aref Pahlevani, Morteza Rafiee, Mehdi Ansari-rad
abstract: We study the Mpemba effect in a pair of linearly coupled harmonic oscillators, one of which is parametrically driven and coupled to an independent thermal bath. Using the covariance-matrix formalism, we derive the relaxation dynamics under both Gaussian white noise and Lorentzian colored noise, including single-channel and dual-channel noise embedding. We characterize relaxation through the Frobenius distance to the steady state and through the projection onto the slowest mode of the dynamical generator. Our results show that parametric driving provides the primary control knob for anomalous relaxation: as the drive approaches the stability boundary, the Mpemba crossing time decreases systematically. Colored noise further enhances the effect, with dual-oscillator Lorentzian noise producing a stronger reduction in the crossing time than single-oscillator noise and enlarging the parameter region where the Mpemba effect occurs. Nevertheless, the slow-mode structure of the drift matrix remains the dominant mechanism, while the influence of colored noise is secondary and mainly quantitative. We show that the Mpemba crossing time decreases as the system approaches the parametric stability boundary and that Lorentzian colored noise enlarges the region in the parametric and coupling strength plane where the Mpemba effect is observed.
summary: This paper explores how parametric driving and colored noise influence the Mpemba effect in coupled oscillators. It finds that while driving is the essential mechanism for anomalous relaxation, colored noise further accelerates this process and widens the parameter range where it can be observed.
main_problem: Investigating the Mpemba effect (anomalous relaxation) in a pair of coupled harmonic oscillators subject to parametric driving and both Markovian (white) and non-Markovian (colored) noise.
main_result: Parametric driving is the primary driver of the Mpemba effect, while colored noise enhances the effect by accelerating relaxation and expanding the parameter space where the effect occurs.
method: The study uses the covariance-matrix formalism, quantum Langevin equations, and the Lyapunov equation, employing spectral decomposition of the drift matrix and Markovian embedding for colored noise.
model_or_system: A pair of linearly coupled harmonic oscillators where one oscillator is parametrically driven and coupled to independent thermal baths (Gaussian white or Lorentzian colored noise).
key_observables: Frobenius distance to the steady state, projection onto the slowest relaxation mode, and the Mpemba crossing time (t*).
important_parameters: Parametric driving amplitude (Lambda), coupling strength (g), noise correlation time, and damping rates.
main_assumptions: The dynamics are assumed to be Gaussian and bosonic, with noise modeled via Lorentzian spectral density.
figures_summary: Phase diagrams showing the Mpemba crossing time (t*) and the Mpemba strength (M) as functions of driving and coupling strength, illustrating the expansion of the effect under colored noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the Mpemba effect in driven systems, defines the coupled oscillator model and noise regimes, details the covariance-matrix and spectral methods, presents results on the influence of driving and noise, and concludes with potential applications.
why_interesting: It provides new insights into how environmental memory (non-Markovianity) and external driving can be used to control anomalous relaxation dynamics in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(color online) Time evolution of the Frobenius distance (D(σ(t)) to the steady state for hot and cold initial states under Gaus- sian white noise, (a) without parametric driving (Λ = 0) and Evo- lution of occupation numbers of the subsystem a show reaching to steady state too, in (b). The same results obtained for Lorentzian col- ored noise. (c) Evolution of occupation numbers of the subsystem a while (Λ = 0.48)

FIG. 2. (color online) Projection amplitudes onto the slowest relax- ation mode (|c1|) for hot and cold initial states under white (left) and colored noise (right). Hotter states have smaller projection ampli- tudes (|c(h) 1 | < |c(c) 1 |), confirming the occurrence of the Mpemba effect.

FIG. 3. (color online) Time evolution of the Frobenius distance D(σ(t)) to the steady state for hot and cold initial states under Gaus- sian white noise and Lorentzian colored noise at Λ = 0.48. (a) and (b) compare white noise with single-oscillator colored noise in the weak (γL = 0.09ωa, gL = 0.32ωa, D0 = 0.3ωa) and mod- erate noise regimes (γL = 0.05ωa, gL = 0.5ωa, D0 = 0.4ωa), respectively. (c) and (d) compare white noise with dual-oscillator colored noise in the weak and moderate noise regimes, respectively. In all cases, colored noise shortens the Mpemba crossing time rela- tive to white noise, with the dual-noise configuration producing the strongest acceleration of relaxation.

FIG. 5. (color online) Mpemba strength, defined as M = |c(h) 1 |/|c(c) 1 |, as a function of the parametric drive Λ for the system under Gaussian white noise (blue circles), double weak Lorentzian colored noise (green circles), and double moderate Lorentzian col- ored noise (orange circles). For most of the stable parameter range, M remains close to unity, indicating that the Mpemba effect is not very strong. However, colored noise extends the Mpemba regime to smaller values of Λ, where the white-noise case does not show a crossing.

FIG. 4. (color online) Mpemba crossing time t∗as a function of the parametric drive Λ under Gaussian white noise and Lorentzian col- ored noise. (a) and (b) show the single-oscillator weak and moderate colored noise cases, respectively, while (c) and (d) show the dual weak and moderate colored noise cases, respectively. Blue circles denote white-noise results and red squares denote colored noise re- sults. Colored noise reduces the crossing time more efficiently than white noise and extends the Mpemba regime toward smaller values of Λ, away from the instability threshold Λc = 0.5.
